note: Stage P3 Track J1 triage (DEV-084) -- supersedes the 29-entry 2026-07-15 (DEV-044) snapshot; A1's live scan now finds 48 pairs (45 original-scope plus a few incidental new leads from the Track J3 entity splits). Every pair below reflects the actual decision applied to the candidate JSON, not a proposal.

merged.reason: Both are the childbirth goddess, child of Zeus and Hera (Hesiod Theogony 869-923, Iliad 11.248) vs child of Zeus alone (Apollodorus 1.3.1-1.3.5, an abbreviated parentage) -- the same well-known goddess under two spellings, already flagged in DEV-044 as the one high-confidence duplicate in the original 29-pair scan.

Both are Heracles's mother, Zeus's mortal consort, attested consistently across hesiod-theogony, homer-iliad, homer-odyssey, ovid-metamorphoses, hesiod-homeric-hymns, and apollodorus-bibliotheca -- an unambiguous cross-source spelling variant (-a/-e feminine ending, the Athena/Athene pattern).

Both are one of the three Fates (Moirai): child of Night (Hesiod Theogony 176-232, 869-923) vs child of Zeus and Themis (Apollodorus 1.3.1-1.3.5, an alternate but equally standard genealogical tradition for the same group of goddesses). Same figure, -os/-us spelling variant plus a contested-parentage claim preserved on the merged entity.
Both are Jason and Hypsipyle's son: even the same source (homer-iliad) spells him 'Euneus' at 7.442-7.464 and 'Euneos' at 23.708-23.752. Same figure, cross-passage spelling inconsistency.

Both are the river-god father of a nymph daughter (Oenone per Apollodorus 3.12.5, matching the earlier-verified Paris/Oenone passage; Hesperia per Ovid 11.749-11.782) -- same river-god role, -us/bare-stem spelling variant across sources.

Perimela has zero extracted relationships or claims; Perimele is the well-attested Ovid figure (Achelous's beloved, turned into an island, 8.590-8.625). No data loss in merging the empty stub into the documented entity.

Both are daughters of the Sun/Helios, sisters of Phaethusa: Homer's Odyssey (12.127-12.164, 'fair-tressed nymphs, Phaethusa and Lampetie') and Ovid's Metamorphoses (2.319-2.366, 'the eldest, Phaethusa... Lampetia... her sister') name the identical sister pair. Same figure, -a/-e spelling variant across sources.

Epicaste itself conflated THREE different women -- resolved via a 3-way split (DEV-084), not a simple merge: the Jocasta/Oedipus's-mother portion (Homer Odyssey 11.271-11.320, 'the mother of Oedipodes, fair Epicaste') merges into Epicasta (Apollodorus's spelling for the same figure, already used for her married_to Laius / parent_of-by-Menoeceus rows); the other two meanings (Calydon's daughter; Augeas's daughter) were split into their own new, disambiguated entities and are NOT part of this merge.

rejected.reason: Different Thespius-daughter mothers of different sons by Heracles: Anthippe->Hippodromus vs Xanthippe (Dorus's daughter, Pleuron's wife, Agenor's mother) -- distinct passages, distinct roles.
Deioneus is Aeolus's son, Cephalus's father; Eioneus is an unrelated Trojan casualty killed by Hector (Iliad). No overlap.
Eriopis is Oileus's wife (Ajax the Lesser's mother, Iliad); Periopis is Pheres's daughter, Patroclus's mother (Apollodorus). Different mothers of different heroes.
Morpheus (god of dreams) and Orpheus (the legendary musician) are well-known, unrelated figures; coincidental spelling similarity only.
Persaeus is a minor Titan (other_god); Perseus is Danae's son, the hero. Type mismatch confirms distinct figures.
Already source-verified and split in DEV-078 (Track J3a): Aeolus is Hellen's son; Aetolus is Endymion's son, four generations later. Confirmed genuinely distinct.
Aepytus is a Messenian king (Cresphontes's son, killed Polyphontes); Epytus is an Alban king in Ovid's Roman-founding king-list. Different narrative traditions.
Amulius has zero extracted relationships (likely the Roman legendary usurper-king, uncle of Romulus/Remus, mentioned only in passing); Mulius is a Trojan-war casualty in the Iliad. No overlap.

Atreus (Pelops's son, king of Mycenae, father of Agamemnon/Menelaus) and Catreus (Minos's son, king of Crete) are well-established distinct figures despite similar spelling -- the exact ambiguous case DEV-044 flagged, now resolved as distinct on priors (no evidence of conflation in the extracted data).
Hyleus is a Calydonian-boar-hunt casualty; Phyleus is Augeas's son, associated with Heracles's labors and later Iliad references. Different figures.
Pereus is a minor genealogical figure (Neaera's husband, Cepheus's father per the Agenor/Porthaon line); Perseus is the hero. Different contexts, no overlap.
Perses is a Titan (Crius's son, 78 relationships/48 claims -- a major distinct figure); Perseus is the hero. Type mismatch, clearly different.

Pteleon has zero extracted relationships (likely a place-name stub -- there is a real town Pteleon in Thessaly per the Iliad Catalogue of Ships); Teleon is Butes's father. Insufficient evidence to merge.
Ammon is a minor casualty in Ovid's Perseus/Phineus wedding-battle scene; Pammon is one of Priam's sons (Iliad/Apollodorus). Different contexts.

Both names attach to multiple different minor Iliad/Odyssey casualties across different passages (a common Homeric pattern of reusing minor names) -- no specific evidence links Enops and Oenops as the same individual.
Already source-verified and split in DEV-082 (Track J3f): Helen of Troy vs Hellen, Deucalion's son (eponym of the Greeks). Confirmed genuinely distinct.
Already source-verified and split in DEV-082 (Track J3f): Helle, Athamas's daughter, vs Hellen, Deucalion's son. Confirmed genuinely distinct.

Both are zero-data stub entities (nymph type, no extracted relationships or claims) -- insufficient evidence either to confirm or refute identity; default to distinct per this project's standing caution against merging without positive evidence.
Hylas is Heracles's companion/beloved (Argonaut myth); Phylas is a king, Astyoche's father (already handled in DEV-081's Menoeceus investigation). Well-known distinct figures.
Medus is Medea's son; Medusa is the Gorgon. Type mismatch (mortal vs monster), unrelated figures.
Molus is a son of Arges/Demonice or Deucalion (genealogical figure); Tmolus is a mountain-god, Omphale's husband. Different contexts, no overlap.

False positive from the shared '(daughter of Thespius)' descriptor suffix -- both names are already correctly disambiguated as distinct daughters of Thespius, each mothering a different son by Heracles.
Ares is the Olympian war god; Dares is a Trojan priest of Hephaestus (Iliad). Type mismatch, unrelated figures.
Azeus has zero extracted relationships; Zeus is the central Olympian figure (456 relationships). No plausible connection -- likely a coincidental fuzzy match on an unrelated minor/stub name.
Borus is Polydora's husband, Menesthius's father (Iliad); Orus attaches to minor, likely-multiple casualty figures across Iliad/Ovid. Different figures.
Dione is a Titan (Aphrodite's mother in some traditions); Ione is a Nereid from Hesiod's catalogue. Type mismatch, different generation and role.
Dius is one of Priam's sons; Odius is a Halizonian leader killed by Agamemnon (Iliad). Different figures.
Dorus is Hellen's son, eponym of the Dorians (well-established); Orus attaches to minor casualty figures. Clearly distinct.

Both are Nereids (daughters of Nereus/Doris) from the same enumeration passages (Apollodorus 1.2.1-1.2.7, Hesiod Theogony 233-269) -- adjacent, similarly-sounding names in an intentional catalogue of ~50 distinctly-named sisters, not a duplication bug.
Hephaestus is the Olympian smith-god; Phaestus is a hero/place-name figure, Borus's father (Iliad). Type mismatch, unrelated.

Horus is Lycaon's son (turned-to-wolf myth, Apollodorus); Orus attaches to minor casualty figures. Different contexts -- the name coincidentally matches the Egyptian god Horus but is unrelated to it in this corpus.
Idas is an Argonaut hero, Marpessa's husband; Midas is the golden-touch king. Well-known distinct figures.
Ilus is the Trojan figure already extensively disambiguated in DEV-080 (Track J3c); Nilus is a river-god personification of the Nile. Type mismatch, unrelated.
Notus is the South Wind god; Otus is one of the Aloadae giants. Type mismatch, unrelated figures.

False positive from the shared '(son of Hercules)' descriptor suffix -- both are named, distinct sons of Hercules by different Thespian daughters per the source list ('by Meline he had Laomedon'; 'by Autonoe, daughter of Pireus, he had Palaemon').

Both plausibly attach to Iliad/Ovid minor-casualty figures (both 'killed_by' claims), but 'Coeranus' itself shows internal signs of covering more than one Iliad casualty (killed by both Odysseus [5.655] and Hector [17.582] in different passages) -- insufficient confidence to merge without first untangling that internal ambiguity. Flagged as a new, deeper lead, not fixed here.

From different catalogues: Plexaura is an Oceanid (Hesiod Theogony 346-403, daughter of Oceanus/Tethys); Plexaure is a Nereid (Apollodorus 1.2.1-1.2.7, daughter of Nereus/Doris). Different categories of sea-nymph, each independently attested by name in their own enumeration.

Epytos is a minor Iliad figure (Periphas's father); Epytus is Ovid's Alban king. Distinct from each other and from Aepytus (the Messenian king) -- three separate 'Ep(y)t(o/u)s'-pattern figures across three different traditions.

Europa (Agenor's daughter, Zeus's abduction victim, Minos's mother) is the famous figure; Europe is one of Danaus's wives, mother of several of his daughters, per the unrelated Danaid myth (Apollodorus 2.1.5). Different women despite near-identical spelling, confirmed via distinct passage context.
Nice is a mortal Thespius daughter (mother of Nicodromus by Heracles); Nike is the primordial goddess of Victory, Styx's daughter (Hesiod Theogony). Type mismatch confirms genuinely distinct figures.
